name: Job search organization
summary:
**Conversation overview**

The user initiated a research project for blogs and newsletters they're developing, providing context about their design practice and job search materials. They requested comprehensive research on two specific topic areas: Fabrication & Making (covering CNC routing, laser cutting, workshop prototyping, material properties, the digital-to-physical gap, and iterative building processes) and Physical-Digital Integration (encompassing responsive environments, sensor-based spatial awareness, real-time AI context processing, location-based entertainment, and interactive installations). 

Claude launched an extended research task that produced a detailed 29,000-word report covering both topic areas. The research included technical specifications for CNC routing and laser cutting capabilities, material behavior analysis, tolerance management strategies, prototyping workflows, responsive environment technologies, sensor systems, edge AI processing capabilities, location-based entertainment market trends, and interactive installation design principles. The report featured real-world case studies from organizations like teamLab, Meow Wolf, Random International, and Disney, along with specific technical details about hardware platforms, software frameworks, and industry best practices.

The user's request demonstrated deep domain expertise in experiential design, fabrication technologies, and physical-digital integration systems. Their focus on practical applications, technical specifications, and current market trends suggests they're creating content for an audience of practitioners and professionals in the experiential design field. The comprehensive nature of their research request indicates they're developing substantial content pieces that require authoritative, technically accurate information spanning both traditional fabrication methods and cutting-edge interactive technologies.

attachments.extracted_content:
I design physical experiences—spaces and installations that respond to people rather than just existing around them. My path started in game design, which is really systems thinking in disguise: how do you guide attention without people feeling guided? How do you make structure feel natural? Those questions translate directly to production design and experiential work.
I approach projects from concept through execution—experience first, then reverse-engineer the build. That process has taught me that the best installations don't announce themselves. They create moments. The technology, the materials, the structure—all of it should disappear into something that just feels right when you're standing in it. I think the industry is moving toward environments that actually know you're there: spaces that shift, respond, adapt. Not in a gimmicky way, but in ways that feel intuitive, almost invisible. The gap right now isn't capability—it's design literacy. We have the tools. What's missing is more people who understand both the experiential intent and the technical path to get there.
That's where game design thinking becomes valuable in physical space. Subtle systems that make a room feel more alive. Feedback loops that reward exploration. Pacing that guides without directing. The principles that make digital experiences feel effortless apply directly to production design and installation—they just haven't been widely adopted yet.



How I Think About Design
I design physical experiences. Started in game design, picked up fabrication along the way, and now I spend most of my time thinking about how spaces can feel more responsive and human.
That's the short version. Here's the longer one.

The Game Design Foundation
I went to school for game design, which sounds like a very specific thing until you realize how broad it actually is. Game design isn't really about games—it's about systems, feedback loops, and how people move through structured experiences. You learn to think about pacing. You learn that the best guidance doesn't feel like guidance at all. You learn that people will always find ways to interact with your work that you didn't anticipate, and that's usually a feature, not a bug.
What game design really taught me was how to think about experience from the user's perspective rather than the creator's. When you're designing a level, you're not thinking about what looks cool from above—you're thinking about what it feels like to be inside it. What do people notice first? Where do their eyes go? What makes them want to move forward versus hang back and explore? These questions apply to way more than video games.
The other thing game design drills into you is the importance of invisible systems. The best games don't feel designed. They feel natural, like you're just exploring and things are happening. But underneath, there's an enormous amount of intentional structure making that experience possible. Environmental storytelling, sight lines, audio cues, lighting that subtly guides attention—all of it working together so the player feels like they're discovering something rather than being led.
That framework stuck with me. I kept noticing it everywhere: in retail spaces, in museums, in theme parks, in well-designed buildings. The same principles apply. The good ones feel effortless. The bad ones feel like you're fighting against the space.

Learning to Build
Somewhere along the way, I taught myself fabrication. CNC routing, laser cutting, general workshop stuff. It started as a practical thing—I wanted to prototype ideas without waiting on someone else—but it ended up changing how I think about design entirely.
When you can build what you design, it keeps you honest. You stop drawing things that look good on paper but don't make sense in reality. You start thinking about materials, tolerances, assembly, weight. You develop an intuition for what's actually achievable versus what's just a nice render.
But more than that, building things connects you to the physical reality of how people interact with objects and spaces. There's something you learn from making a hundred small things that you can't learn from designing on a screen. How surfaces feel. How edges catch light. How weight distribution affects perception. The gap between a prototype and a finished piece, and what it takes to close that gap.
I have a workshop now. It's not the most interesting thing about me, but it matters because it means I'm not just talking about ideas. I can make them real, test them, iterate on them. That changes the kind of work I'm able to do.

Where Physical and Digital Meet
The thing I keep coming back to is the intersection of physical space and digital systems. Not in a flashy, screens-everywhere way—more in a subtle, how-can-technology-make-a-space-feel-more-alive way.
We've gotten really good at designing digital experiences. Two decades of UX research, A/B testing, and iteration have made screens remarkably intuitive. But physical spaces haven't evolved at the same pace. Most buildings, most retail environments, most public spaces are still basically static. They don't respond to you. They don't adapt. They just exist.
That's starting to change. Sensors are cheap now. Compute is cheap. AI can process context in real time. AR is becoming practical. The tools exist to make physical environments that actually respond to the people in them—not in a gimmicky way, but in ways that feel natural and enhance the experience of being there.
I'm interested in what happens when you apply game design thinking to those kinds of spaces. When you treat a physical environment like a system that can be designed for flow, discovery, and engagement. When the room knows something about who's in it and can respond accordingly.
This isn't science fiction anymore. Companies are already doing pieces of it. Location-based entertainment, interactive installations, smart retail environments, immersive theater. The technology is there. What's often missing is the design thinking to make it feel cohesive and human rather than like a tech demo.

What I Actually Care About
If I'm being honest, the technology is the least interesting part. Sensors and AI and AR are just tools. What actually interests me is the human experience on the other end.
How do you make someone feel welcome in a space? How do you create moments of surprise or delight without it feeling forced? How do you guide attention without people feeling manipulated? How do you make technology disappear into the experience rather than being the point of the experience?
These are design questions, not technology questions. And they're the same questions game designers have been wrestling with for decades. The difference is the medium. Instead of pixels and polygons, it's physical materials, lighting, spatial relationships, and the systems that connect them.
I think about this stuff probably more than I should. The details that make a space feel considered versus thrown together. Why some environments make you want to linger and others make you want to leave. What creates a sense of presence, of being somewhere that matters.

Where I'm At
Right now I'm based in LA, working on projects that let me apply this kind of thinking. Some fabrication, some design, some of the weirder intersection stuff when I can find it.
I don't have all the answers figured out. This field—if you can even call it a field—is still taking shape. The people doing the most interesting work are usually pulling from multiple disciplines: architecture, theater, game design, industrial design, interaction design, engineering. Nobody has a perfect playbook.
What I do have is a way of thinking about experience that came from game design, the ability to actually build things, and genuine curiosity about where physical and digital design are heading. That combination feels increasingly relevant as the line between the two keeps blurring.
If you're working on something in this space, or thinking about it, I'm always happy to talk. Not in a networking way—just because I find it genuinely interesting and there aren't that many people exploring it yet.

Based in LA. Game design background. Fabrication workshop. Thinking about responsive environments and how spaces can do more.
Core Topics & Disciplines
Game Design Framework
* Systems thinking and feedback loops
* Environmental storytelling and spatial design
* Pacing and player/user guidance
* Invisible systems that feel natural
* Experience-first design methodology
Fabrication & Making
* CNC routing and laser cutting
* Workshop-based prototyping
* Material properties and tolerances
* Gap between digital design and physical reality
* Iterative building process
Physical-Digital Integration
* Responsive environments and adaptive spaces
* Sensor-based spatial awareness
* Real-time AI context processing
* Location-based entertainment
* Interactive installations
Spatial Experience Design
* Production design and experiential work
* Flow and discovery in physical spaces
* Sight lines and attention guidance
* How people move through structured experiences
* Creating presence and moments
Key Insights
On Design Principles
* Best installations don't announce themselves—they create moments
* Technology should disappear into the experience
* Good spaces feel effortless; bad ones feel like you're fighting them
* The gap isn't capability—it's design literacy
* Guidance that doesn't feel like guidance
On the Industry
* Physical spaces haven't evolved at the pace of digital UX
* Most environments are still static and non-responsive
* The tools exist; what's missing is people who understand both experiential intent and technical execution
* The field is still taking shape—no perfect playbook yet
* Most interesting work pulls from multiple disciplines
On Process
* Building what you design keeps you honest
* You learn things from making that you can't learn on screen
* Experience first, then reverse-engineer the build
* People will always interact in unanticipated ways—that's a feature
On Technology
* Technology is the least interesting part—human experience matters
* Sensors, AI, AR are just tools
* The questions are design questions, not technology questions
* Not about screens everywhere—about subtle enhancement
On Transferable Skills
* Game design questions apply directly to physical space
* Digital experience principles translate to production design
* Prototype thinking from game development applies to spatial work
* Systems thinking in disguise
Professional Positioning
* Intersection of game design, fabrication, and experiential spaces
* Concept through execution capability
* Based in LA with active workshop
* Interest in where physical and digital design are heading
* Multi-disciplinary approach (architecture, theater, game design, industrial design, interaction design, engineering)
